statement.en: Supervised Learning Has a Necessary Geometric Blind Spot: Theory, Consequences, and Minimal Repair
statement.summary:
We prove that empirical risk minimisation (ERM) imposes a necessary geometric constraint on learned representations: any encoder that minimises supervised loss must retain non-zero Jacobian sensitivity in directions that are label-correlated in training data but nuisance at test time. This is not a contingent failure of current methods; it is a mathematical consequence of the supervised objective itself. We call this the geometric blind spot of supervised learning (Theorem 1), and show it holds across proper scoring rules, architectures, and dataset sizes.
  This single theorem unifies four lines of prior empirical work that were previously treated separately: non-robust predictive features, texture bias, corruption fragility, and the robustness-accuracy tradeoff. In this framing, adversar

reiTyping.dfumt8Reason: The axiom presents a novel theoretical claim (Theorem 1) with broad unifying scope, but the preprint date (2026-04-25) is a future date relative to standard knowledge cutoffs, and the abstract is incomplete (cuts off mid-sentence). The claim appears internally coherent and addresses a real phenomenon, but requires peer review validation and complete presentation. The unification of four empirical phenomena is substantive but the proof details are not accessible for independent verification within this submission.

ramanujanFormula.interpretation: 教師あり学習は、訓練で相関した方向を永遠に見失う運命にある。

visualGlyph.interpretation: 中心の盲点は必然：訓練で光った軸ほど、テスト時の暗闇へ向く。
